posts.title_en: Lion's Mane: Our Most Talked-About Mushroom
posts.excerpt_en: It looks like nothing else, tastes like the sea, and sells out every week. Here is how we grow it and how to cook it properly.
posts.body_en:
Lion's Mane (Hericium erinaceus) is one of the most recognisable mushrooms in the world 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 there is nothing else that looks like it. The long white cascading spines grow outward from a single base, resembling a waterfall frozen in place. In the wild it grows on wounded or dying hardwood trees. In our grow room, we produce it year-round on supplemented sawdust blocks.

We first added Lion's Mane to our lineup in July of our first year. It was the most requested variety by restaurants 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 chefs had been reading about it and wanted to work with it. The first flush surprised us: blocks that looked dormant for three weeks suddenly pinned overnight. Now we run it on a three-week cycle and harvest every block by hand, pulling the whole fruiting body when the spines reach about 2 cm.

### What it tastes like

The flavour is mild and sweet, with a dense, meaty texture that holds up well in a hot pan. Many people compare it to lobster or crab 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 not in taste exactly, but in the way it absorbs butter and gives back something rich and satisfying. Slice it thick, at least 1.5 cm, and press it flat in a dry pan over medium-high heat for 3脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聯4 minutes per side without moving it. The outside caramelises; the inside stays juicy.

We sell it in 150g packs 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 enough for two people as a side or one as a main. It is by far our best-selling variety among home cooks who have tried it once and come back every week.

posts.title_en: Spring Flush 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 Our Best Blue Oyster Harvest Yet
posts.excerpt_en: April brought ideal humidity and the biggest Blue Oyster flush we have seen. Here is what happened and what we changed to make it happen.
posts.body_en:
Every grower waits for a flush like this. The week of April 21st, the humidity settled into a perfect 88% and temperatures held steady at 16脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聜脙聝脗聜脙聜脗掳C 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 and the Blue Oyster responded. Within four days, every substrate block in the growing room had pinned simultaneously.

We harvested just over 60 kg across three days, our highest single-variety output since we opened. The clusters were dense and uniform, caps thin enough to saut脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗漏 in under two minutes. We rushed a batch to Kauppahalli on the Tuesday morning and it sold out before noon.

### What made the difference

We made one substrate change in February 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 adding 15% spent coffee grounds sourced from a caf脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗漏 on Kauppakatu. The nitrogen boost shortens colonisation time and improves cap density during fruiting. It also cuts our substrate cost slightly.

We will repeat the same mix for the summer blocks and track the results. If the pattern holds, we plan to increase Blue Oyster production by about 30% before autumn.

posts.title_en: Why We Switched to Compostable Packaging
posts.excerpt_en: Plastic clamshells were the easy choice. Here is why we changed, what we changed to, and what it actually costs us.
posts.body_en:
When we started, we used standard plastic clamshells. They are cheap, easy to stack, and keep moisture in. Most of the mushroom industry uses them. We used them for the first six months.

The problem was not cost or logistics 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 it was consistency. We kept telling customers our mushrooms were grown with no chemicals, on local substrate, with the forest in mind. And then we handed them a single-use plastic container. It did not add up.

### What we use now

We now use kraft paper bags with a small ventilation window for loose mushrooms, and flat cardboard trays with a paper band for gift packs. Both are fully compostable in a home compost. The bags come from a printer in Tampere; the trays from a supplier here in Jyv脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗陇skyl脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗陇.

Shelf life is slightly shorter 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 about 4 days versus 6 in plastic. We handle this by harvesting to order as much as possible and delivering more frequently. Several customers have mentioned the packaging specifically as a reason they keep buying from us.

Ongelma ei ollut kustannuksissa tai logistiikassa 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 se oli johdonmukaisuus. posts.excerpt_en: Enoki loves low temperatures 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 which makes it a natural fit for Central Finland. We added it to the lineup in January.
posts.body_en:
Enoki (Flammulina velutipes) is unusual among cultivated mushrooms: it fruits best in cold. While most of our varieties prefer 16脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聯20脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聜脙聝脗聜脙聜脗掳C, enoki pins most reliably between 8脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聯13脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聜脙聝脗聜脙聜脗掳C. For a farm in Central Finland, that is not a problem 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 it is an advantage.

We added the first enoki blocks in January and had our first harvest by late February. The clusters came out tight and white 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 exactly what you want. Long thin stems, tiny caps, a faint sweetness that is almost impossible to get right unless the temperature is low during fruiting.

### What to do with it

Most people know enoki from ramen, where it goes in at the last second. But it is also excellent raw in salads 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 it stays crunchy even when dressed. Our favourite preparation: bundle a small handful, wrap tightly in a strip of bacon, and grill over medium heat. The bacon fat bastes the mushroom from outside; the inside stays crisp. Takes 10 minutes.

posts.title_en: How a Single Grow Room Became a Farm
posts.excerpt_en: We started with one room, one variety, and no customers. Twelve months later we supply five markets across Jyv脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗陇skyl脙聝脗聝脙聜脗聝脙聝脗聜脙聜脗陇.
posts.body_en:
The first grow room was a converted storage space 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 about 12 square metres, a second-hand humidity controller, and 20 substrate blocks of Blue Oyster. We had read everything we could find about mushroom cultivation and still had no real idea what we were doing.

The first flush came after 18 days. Small clusters, a little pale from inconsistent light, but unmistakably real. We harvested about 3 kg and gave most of it away to neighbours and a restaurant on Kauppakatu who agreed to try it. The chef called back two days later to order more.

### Year one in numbers

We expanded to three rooms by May and added Lion's Mane in July. By December we were running five varieties across 80 substrate blocks per cycle. Total harvest for 2026: approximately 420 kg across all varieties.

The hardest part was not cultivation 脙聝脗聝脙聜脗垄脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聙脙聝脗聜脙聜脗聰 it was consistency. Customers and restaurants need to know supply will be there every week. Building that reliability took most of the year. We now harvest on a rolling two-week cycle per room, which keeps deliveries steady regardless of which blocks are peaking.

We still operate from the same building. The first grow room is still running, and it still produces our best Blue Oyster. There is something about a room that has been used for a while that makes the environment settle in.
